<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define cloud computing. List any **four essential characteristics** as per the NIST definition.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications, services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort.
- **Four characteristics (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. On-demand self-service
  2. Broad network access
  3. Resource pooling
  4. Rapid elasticity
  5. Measured service *(any four)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** with one sensor-based example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Model | Provider Manages | You Manage | Sensor Example |
|-------|------------------|------------|----------------|
| **IaaS** | Hardware, hypervisor | OS, runtime, app | Run Mosquitto MQTT broker on AWS EC2 |
| **PaaS** | Hardware → Runtime | App + data | Use AWS IoT Core for sensor ingestion |
| **SaaS** | Everything | User data only | Visualize sensor data on ThingSpeak |

*(0.5 mark for each row + 0.5 for distinction clarity = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Cloud Computing Questions**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse service models with deployment models** — IaaS/PaaS/SaaS ≠ Public/Private/Hybrid. This single mix-up can cost 4–5 marks.
> 2. **Always draw the cloud stack diagram** in IaaS/PaaS/SaaS questions. Examiners allocate at least 2 marks for a clear diagram.
> 3. **Numerical problems must show unit conversion and currency choice** explicitly. A bare answer of "₹13,195" without steps scores 0, not 14.
> 4. **For "advantages over traditional" questions**, mention CAPEX → OPEX shift, elasticity, and global reach — at least *three* points.
> 5. **Do not write "cloud is just internet"** — this is a very common 2-mark deduction.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **three service models** of cloud computing (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) with a neat diagram. Discuss how each is used in a typical sensor-based IoT application.

**(b) [7 marks]** A startup deploys **10,000 smart energy meters** sending data every **5 minutes** to a cloud IoT platform. Compute the **monthly ingestion cost** if the platform charges **₹0.07 per million messages**. Also calculate the **annual ingestion cost**. State all assumptions.

**Model Answer — Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
- **Diagram of stack (2 marks):** Must show IaaS at the bottom, PaaS in the middle, SaaS at top — with the "managed by provider" boundary clearly indicated.
- **IaaS (1.5 marks):** Define, give examples (EC2), and sensor use case (running own MQTT broker on a VM for 10,000 sensors).
- **PaaS (1.5 marks):** Define, give examples (AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub), and sensor use case (managed ingestion, routing rules, device shadows).
- **SaaS (1.5 marks):** Define, give examples (ThingSpeak, Blynk, Google Sheets integration), and sensor use case (ready dashboard for end users to view sensor data).
- **Comparison summary (0.5 mark):** Higher abstraction = less control + more convenience.

**Part (b) — 7 marks (Step-by-Step):**

*Step 1: Messages per meter per day*

$$
\frac{24 \times 60}{5} = 288 \text{ messages/day}
$$

*Step 2: Messages per meter per month*

$$
288 \times 30 = 8{,}640 \text{ messages/month}
$$

*Step 3: Total monthly messages*

$$
8{,}640 \times 10{,}000 = 86.4 \times 10^6 = 86.4 \text{ million messages}
$$

*Step 4: Monthly ingestion cost*

$$
C_{ingest} = 86.4 \times 0.07 = \text{₹} 6.048
$$

*Step 5: Annual ingestion cost*

$$
C_{annual} = 6.048 \times 12 = \text{₹} 72.576
$$

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- All messages are chargeable (no free tier)
- 30-day month
- No compression or batching applied

> [!NOTE]
> The cost is surprisingly low — this is intentional. **Cloud economics favors IoT workloads** because the per-message price is sub-paisa. Examiners love this kind of "intuitive-feel" answer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **four deployment models** of cloud computing. Mention at least **two advantages** and **one limitation** of the **public cloud** specifically.

**(b) [7 marks]** An organization is replacing its on-premise server (₹15,00,000 upfront + ₹1,80,000/year power) with a public cloud costing ₹4,50,000/year operationally. Calculate the **3-year TCO for both options**, the **net savings**, and the **percentage ROI** of migration.

**Model Answer — Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
| Model | Description | Sensor Example (1 mark each = 4 marks) |
|-------|-------------|---------------------------------------|
| Public | Third-party owned, public access | Smart-city air sensors on AWS |
| Private | Single organization | Hospital vitals on on-prem private cloud |
| Hybrid | Mix of public + private | Sensitive patient data on private, analytics on public |
| Community | Shared by similar orgs | KAU + farmer cooperatives sharing agri sensors |

**Public cloud advantages (2 marks):**
- Low upfront cost (no CAPEX)
- Massive scalability for sensor bursts
- Global CDN for low-latency sensor data delivery
- Managed services (AI/ML, DB, queue)

**Public cloud limitation (1 mark):**
- Data residency and sovereignty concerns — important for sensitive sensor data (e.g., defense, medical).

**Part (b) — 7 marks (Step-by-Step):**

*Step 1: 3-year on-premise TCO*

$$
C_{op}^{3yr} = 15{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}80{,}000 \times 3) = 15{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}40{,}000 = 20{,}40{,}000
$$

*Step 2: 3-year cloud TCO*

$$
C_{cloud}^{3yr} = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 3 = 13{,}50{,}000
$$

*Step 3: Net savings*

$$
S = 20{,}40{,}000 - 13{,}50{,}000 = 6{,}90{,}000
$$

*Step 4: ROI*

$$
ROI = \frac{6{,}90{,}000}{13{,}50{,}000} \times 100\% = 51.11\%
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Migration to public cloud yields **51.11% ROI** over 3 years and saves **₹6.9 lakhs**, demonstrating strong economic justification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Last-Minute Revision — KTU PCCIT502 Module 1: Cloud Computing**

- ✅ **Definition (NIST):** On-demand, shared pool, configurable, rapid provisioning, minimal management.
- ✅ **5 Characteristics:** On-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service.
- ✅ **3 Service Models (stack order, low → high):** IaaS → PaaS → SaaS. *Higher = less control, more convenience.*
- ✅ **4 Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- ✅ **Sensor-Cloud Stack Layers (must memorize in order):** Perception → Gateway → Ingestion → Processing → Storage → Analytics → Application.
- ✅ **Key cost formulas to memorize:**
  - $C_{cpu} = N_{vCPU} \times H \times P_{h}$
  - $C_{sto} = S_{GB} \times M \times P_{GB\text{-}m}$
  - $C_{out} = D_{GB} \times P_{out}$
  - $ROI = \dfrac{Gain - Cost}{Cost} \times 100\%$
  - $PUE = \dfrac{Total\ Power}{IT\ Power}$ (ideal $\leq 1.5$)
  - $E_{CO_2} = P \times t \times CI_{grid}$
- ✅ **Common sensor protocols to the cloud:** MQTT (most common), HTTP/HTTPS, CoAP (constrained devices), AMQP.
- ✅ **IoT cloud platforms (must know examples):** AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT, ThingSpeak, Blynk.
- ✅ **Three common exam traps:**
  1. Service models vs deployment models confusion.
  2. Forgetting unit conversions in cost problems.
  3. Writing "cloud = internet" — never say this.
- ✅ **For numericals, always state 3 things:** (i) Assumptions, (ii) Formula used, (iii) Unit in the final answer.
- ✅ **For diagrams:** Draw the IaaS-PaaS-SaaS stack — it is worth 2–3 marks on its own in any 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT502 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Embedded Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **Embedded System** is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based, software-driven, dedicated-purpose computing system that is designed to perform a specific, predefined task or a tightly constrained set of functions, embedded as an integral part of a larger mechanical, electrical, or electronic device. Unlike a general-purpose computer, it is *not* user-programmable for arbitrary applications; its operational behavior is largely fixed at design time through firmware.

The three mandatory pillars of an embedded system, as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme PCCIT502 syllabus, are:

1. **Sensing** (interaction with the physical world through sensors — directly maps to this course).
2. **Processing** (computation through microcontrollers, DSPs, ASICs, FPGAs, SoCs).
3. **Actuation / Communication** (driving outputs, displays, or transmitting data).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Smart Kitchen Toaster"**
> Imagine a modern pop-up toaster. You drop bread in, press a lever, and it toasts. Inside is a tiny **microcontroller** running a single program whose only job is: (a) read a *temperature sensor* (NTC thermistor), (b) compute elapsed time using an internal *timer*, and (c) drive a *solenoid actuator* to pop the toast up when the browning threshold is met. The toaster cannot browse the internet, run Excel, or play music. It is dedicated. It is *embedded*. Now scale that idea to a Boeing 787 (millions of lines of code, hundreds of embedded processors) — same principle, vastly higher complexity.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canonical Embedded-System Design Metrics (per KTU / Marilyn Wolf, *Computers as Components*):**
> - **NRE Cost (Non-Recurring Engineering Cost)** — one-time design/fabrication cost. **Measured in USD (or ₹)**.
> - **Unit Cost** — recurring per-piece manufacturing cost. **Measured in USD / INR**.
> - **Performance** — execution time of a defined task. **Measured in seconds (s) or cycles**.
> - **Power Dissipation** — energy consumed per unit time. **Measured in Watts (W)**.
> - **Time-to-Market** — wall-clock duration from concept to shipment. **Measured in months**.
> - **Reliability / MTBF** — Mean Time Between Failures. **Measured in hours (h)**.
> - **Safety** — probability of causing harm under fault. **Often quantified probabilistically (10⁻⁹ failures/hour for avionics, DO-178C)**.
> - **Availability** — fraction of time system is operational. **Unitless, 0 ≤ A ≤ 1**.

### 1.4 Embedded vs General-Purpose Computing — The KTU Differentiation

| Parameter | General-Purpose Computer (PC) | Embedded System |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-----------------|
| Purpose | Multi-purpose, user-defined | Single, dedicated function |
| User Awareness of OS | High (Windows/Linux/macOS) | Often none (firmware only) |
| Power Budget | ~65–1000 W | **mW to few W** |
| Cost | ₹30,000 – ₹3,00,000+ | **₹50 – ₹5,000** per node |
| Upgrade Cycle | 3–5 years | **10–20 years** (industrial) |
| Real-Time Constraint | Soft (best-effort) | **Hard / Firm / Soft** (mandatory) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars — Deconstructed

**(A) Hardware Core (Processor)**
The "brain" of the system. Common choices:
- **Microcontroller (MCU)** — e.g., ATmega328P, STM32F4, ESP32, PIC16F877A. CPU + RAM + ROM + peripherals on one die.
- **Microprocessor (MPU)** — e.g., ARM Cortex-A53. Needs external RAM/ROM.
- **DSP (Digital Signal Processor)** — optimized for MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) operations. Used in audio/radar.
- **ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit)** — silicon hardwired for one job (e.g., MP3 decoder). Highest performance, highest NRE.
- **FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array)** — reconfigurable logic fabric. Used in prototyping and low-volume high-perf systems.
- **SoC (System-on-Chip)** — heterogeneous integration: CPU + GPU + DSP + radio. Found in smartphones, IoT.

**(B) Software (Firmware)**
- **Bare-metal firmware** — direct register-level C/assembly. No OS.
- **RTOS (Real-Time Operating System)** — e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX. Provides scheduling, IPC, semaphores.
- **Linux (Embedded)** — e.g., Yocto, Buildroot. For high-end gateways.
- **Application-layer** — domain logic (control law, signal processing).

**(C) Sensors & Actuators** *(Most relevant to PCCIT502)*
- **Sensors** convert physical → electrical signals (temperature, pressure, light, motion).
- **Actuators** convert electrical → physical action (motors, solenoids, LEDs, buzzers).

### 2.2 The Embedded System Design Flow

1. **Requirement Specification** — Functional + non-functional (timing, power, cost).
2. **Architecture Selection** — MCU/MPU/SoC, OS/RTOS choice.
3. **Hardware–Software Partitioning** — what goes in silicon vs software.
4. **Detailed Design** — schematic, PCB layout, firmware modules.
5. **Implementation & Integration** — coding, fabrication.
6. **Testing & Validation** — unit, integration, system, acceptance.
7. **Deployment & Maintenance** — OTA updates, field returns analysis.

### 2.3 Real-Time Classifications — Critical for Sensors Course

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Real-Time:** Missing a deadline is a **total system failure** (e.g., airbag deployment, anti-lock braking).
> **Firm Real-Time:** Late result has **no value** but no damage (e.g., stock-tick display).
> **Soft Real-Time:** Late result has **degraded utility** (e.g., video streaming, audio playback).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{exec} = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clk}}$ | Execution time from cycle count and clock | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $CPI = \dfrac{\sum (IC_i \cdot CPI_i)}{IC_{total}}$ | Average Cycles Per Instruction | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | CPU time (three-factor model) | seconds |
| 4 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Dynamic power dissipation (CMOS) | Watts (W) |
| 5 | $E_{bit} = V_{DD}^{2} \cdot C$ | Energy per bit transition | Joules (J) |
| 6 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Availability | dimensionless (0..1) |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{C_s}{P_s}}$ | Speedup (Amdahl's, single resource) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{static}$ | Total CMOS power = dynamic + leakage | Watts (W) |
| 9 | $C_{unit} = C_{NRE}/N + C_{rec}$ | Per-unit cost for N units | ₹/USD |
| 10 | $t_{resp}^{max} \leq D$ | Real-time deadline constraint (hard RT) | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equation (4) is the single most exploited lever in low-power embedded design — every **0.1 V drop in $V_{DD}$** approximately **saves 19% dynamic power** at constant $f$ (since $P \propto V_{DD}^{2}$). This is why IoT sensor nodes aggressively voltage-scale during sleep.

### 2.5 Design Trade-offs — The "Iron Triangle"

An embedded engineer can optimize at most **two** of the following three simultaneously:
- **Performance** (speed, latency).
- **Power** (battery life, thermal).
- **Cost** (silicon area, BOM).

This is the **Performance–Power–Cost triangle** and it is a guaranteed KTU short-answer/essay item.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — CPU Execution Time (KTU Numerical Pattern)

**Problem:** A sensor-fusion MCU runs a Kalman filter. The compiled code contains $IC = 2 \times 10^{6}$ instructions, with a weighted average $CPI = 1.4$. The crystal oscillator drives the core at $f_{clk} = 50\,\text{MHz}$. Compute the wall-clock execution time.

**Step 1 — Identify the three factors.**

$$IC = 2 \times 10^{6}, \quad CPI = 1.4, \quad f_{clk} = 50 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Convert clock frequency to clock period.**

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}}\,\text{s} = 2 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{s} = 20\,\text{ns}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the three-factor CPU time equation.**

$$T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerically.**

$$T_{CPU} = (2 \times 10^{6}) \times (1.4) \times (2 \times 10^{-8})\,\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Group terms to simplify safely.**

$$T_{CPU} = (2 \times 1.4 \times 2) \times (10^{6} \times 10^{-8})\,\text{s} = (5.6) \times (10^{-2})\,\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Final value.**

$$\boxed{T_{CPU} = 5.6 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{s} = 56\,\text{ms}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 56 ms filter cycle is comfortable for a 50 Hz IMU (period 20 ms is the bottleneck) only if the rest of the pipeline fits. This is precisely why sensor-fusion MCUs use hardware accelerators (CMSIS-DSP) — to drop $IC$ by 5–10×.

---

### 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — Dynamic Power Reduction by Voltage Scaling

**Problem:** A wearable health-monitor SoC currently operates at $V_{DD} = 1.2\,\text{V}$, clock $f = 100\,\text{MHz}$, effective switched capacitance $C = 10\,\text{nF}$, and activity factor $\alpha = 0.2$. Compute the dynamic power. If the firmware is rewritten to operate at $V_{DD}' = 0.9\,\text{V}$ (and $f$ is unchanged), what is the new dynamic power and the percentage saving?

**Step 1 — Recall the CMOS dynamic power formula.**

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$

**Step 2 — Substitute initial values (SI units: $C = 10 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{F}$).**

$$P_{dyn} = (0.2)(10 \times 10^{-9})(1.2)^{2}(100 \times 10^{6})\,\text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the squares and products.**

$$P_{dyn} = (0.2)(10 \times 10^{-9})(1.44)(10^{8})\,\text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-9+8}\,\text{W} = 0.288 \times 10^{-1}\,\text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{dyn} = 28.8\,\text{mW}}$$

**Step 5 — Recompute at $V_{DD}' = 0.9\,\text{V}$.**

$$P_{dyn}' = (0.2)(10 \times 10^{-9})(0.9)^{2}(100 \times 10^{6})\,\text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate.**

$$P_{dyn}' = (0.2)(10 \times 10^{-9})(0.81)(10^{8})\,\text{W} = 0.162 \times 10^{-1}\,\text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{dyn}' = 16.2\,\text{mW}}$$

**Step 7 — Percentage reduction.**

$$\Delta P\,\% = \frac{P_{dyn} - P_{dyn}'}{P_{dyn}} \times 100 = \frac{28.8 - 16.2}{28.8} \times 100$$

$$\Delta P\,\% = \frac{12.6}{28.8} \times 100 = 43.75\,\%$$

**Step 8 — Theoretical cross-check (since $\alpha, C, f$ unchanged):**

$$\frac{P_{dyn}'}{P_{dyn}} = \left(\frac{V_{DD}'}{V_{DD}}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{0.9}{1.2}\right)^{2} = (0.75)^{2} = 0.5625$$

So $P_{dyn}' = 0.5625 \times 28.8 = 16.2\,\text{mW}$ ✓ — saving of $1 - 0.5625 = 43.75\,\%$ ✓.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 25% voltage drop ($1.2 \to 0.9\,\text{V}$) yields a **~44% power saving**. This non-linear quadratic relationship is the *fundamental* justification for Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS) in modern sensor-node SoCs.

---

### 3.3 Worked Problem 3 — Amdahl's Law (Speed-up Limited by Serial Fraction)

**Problem:** A digital-signal-processing kernel spends 80% of its time on a loop that is parallelized onto 4 hardware accelerators. The remaining 20% is inherently serial. Compute the speed-up and the *theoretical* maximum speed-up with infinite accelerators.

**Step 1 — Amdahl's Law (single-resource form):**

$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - P_s) + \dfrac{P_s}{N}}$$

where $P_s$ = parallelizable fraction, $N$ = number of accelerators, $(1 - P_s)$ = serial fraction.

**Step 2 — Substitute $P_s = 0.8$, $1 - P_s = 0.2$, $N = 4$.**

$$S = \frac{1}{0.2 + \dfrac{0.8}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.2} = \frac{1}{0.4}$$

$$\boxed{S = 2.5\times}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical maximum as $N \to \infty$:**

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - P_s} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5\times$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even with infinite hardware, we are capped at **5×** because of the 20% serial tax. This is *the* reason embedded architects obsess over reducing serial code (interrupts, locks, shared memory) — the speed-up is mathematically bounded.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Processor for a Sensor Node

| Sensor Application | Sample Rate | Typical Processor | Why? |
|-------------------|-------------|---------------------|------|
| Room thermostat | 1 Hz | 8-bit MCU (PIC, ATtiny) | Ultra-low power, < ₹20 BOM |
| Wearable heart-rate (PPG) | 100 Hz | Cortex-M0+ / M4 | DSP extensions, low active mA |
| MEMS IMU fusion (drone) | 1 kHz | Cortex-M4F + FPU | Hard RT, floating-point math |
| Automotive radar | 20 MHz IF | DSP + FPGA | Massive MAC throughput |
| Smart camera (vision) | 30–60 fps | SoC (Cortex-A + NPU) | CNN inference, Linux needed |
| Industrial PLC scan | 10 ms cycle | Cortex-R5 (lockstep) | Functional safety, ISO 26262 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Block-Level Architecture of an Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[PHYSICAL WORLD] --> nodeS[SENSOR]
    nodeS --> nodeSG[SIGNAL CONDITIONING]
    nodeSG --> nodeADC[ADC]
    nodeADC --> nodeMCU[MCU / PROCESSOR]
    nodeMCU --> nodeDAC[DAC]
    nodeDAC --> nodeDR[DRIVER]
    nodeDR --> nodeACT[ACTUATOR]
    nodeACT --> nodeP
    nodeMCU --> nodeCOM[COMM INTERFACE]
    nodeCOM --> nodeEXT[EXTERNAL SYSTEM / CLOUD]
    nodeMEM[FLASH + RAM] -.-> nodeMCU
    nodeOS[RTOS / FIRMWARE] -.-> nodeMCU
    nodePWR[POWER MANAGEMENT] --- nodeS
    nodePWR --- nodeMCU
    nodePWR --- nodeACT
    nodeCLK[CLOCK / OSCILLATOR] --- nodeMCU
    nodeUSR[USER INTERFACE] --- nodeMCU
    classDef hw fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ext fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
    class nodeS,nodeSG,nodeADC,nodeDAC,nodeDR,nodeACT,nodeCOM hw
    class nodeMCU,nodeMEM,nodeOS,nodePWR,nodeCLK,nodeUSR proc
    class nodeP,nodeEXT ext
```

**Reading Guide:** The **physical world** is sensed → conditioned → digitized → processed → the decision drives an **actuator** that alters the physical world (closed loop). Memory, OS, power, clock, and user interface all *support* the processor.

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Embedded Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeES[EMBEDDED SYSTEMS] --> nodeA[BY PERFORMANCE]
    nodeES --> nodeB[BY TIMING CONSTRAINT]
    nodeES --> nodeC[BY TRIGGERING]
    nodeA --> nodeA1[Small Scale 8 16 bit MCU]
    nodeA --> nodeA2[Medium Scale 32 bit MCU MPU]
    nodeA --> nodeA3[Large Scale SoC FPGA]
    nodeA --> nodeA4[Ultra Large Multi SoC Server]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Hard Real Time]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Firm Real Time]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Soft Real Time]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Event Driven Reactive]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Time Triggered Cyclic]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Hybrid]
    classDef root fill:#FDE68A,stroke:#92400E,stroke-width:3px
    classDef lvl1 fill:#BFDBFE,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef lvl2 fill:#FECACA,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:2px
    class nodeES root
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC lvl1
    class nodeA1,nodeA2,nodeA3,nodeA4,nodeB1,nodeB2,nodeB3,nodeC1,nodeC2,nodeC3 lvl2
```

### 4.3 The Hardware–Software Co-Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR[REQUIREMENTS] --> nodeSP[SPECIFICATION]
    nodeSP --> nodePART[HARDWARE SOFTWARE PARTITIONING]
    nodePART --> nodeHW[HW DESIGN RTL SCHEMATIC PCB]
    nodePART --> nodeSW[SW DESIGN FIRMWARE RTOS]
    nodeHW --> nodeINT[INTEGRATION ON TARGET]
    nodeSW --> nodeINT
    nodeINT --> nodeVER[VERIFICATION TEST]
    nodeVER --> nodeCERT[VALIDATION CERTIFICATION]
    nodeCERT --> nodePROD[PRODUCTION DEPLOYMENT]
    nodePROD --> nodeMNT[FIELD MAINTENANCE UPDATES]
    classDef k fill:#FBCFE8,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px
    classDef v fill:#A7F3D0,stroke:#065F46,stroke-width:2px
    class nodeR,nodeSP,nodePART k
    class nodeHW,nodeSW,nodeINT,nodeVER,nodeCERT,nodePROD,nodeMNT v
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The *partitioning* block is where the architectural decision (ASIC vs FPGA vs general-purpose MCU; bare-metal vs RTOS vs Linux) is *sealed*. Mistakes here propagate to the right and become exponentially expensive.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Set A, Q1a]**

**Q1. (a) Define an embedded system. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from a general-purpose computer.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
An embedded system is a microprocessor/microcontroller-based, dedicated-purpose computing engine that is an integral part of a larger device, designed to perform a *specific* function in real time.

Four distinguishing characteristics:

1. **Single-purpose / dedicated function** — not user-reprogrammable for arbitrary tasks.
2. **Tight constraints** on power, cost, size, and timing (often real-time).
3. **Reactive / real-time operation** — must respond to external events within a bounded deadline.
4. **High reliability & long lifecycle** — industrial embedded products have 10–20 year field lives with near-zero human intervention.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Q1b]**

**Q1. (b) Differentiate between Hard, Firm, and Soft real-time systems with one sensor-based example each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Class | Late-Result Consequence | Sensor Example |
|-------|--------------------------|----------------|
| **Hard Real-Time** | Catastrophic system failure / loss of life | Airbag crash sensor — must fire within < 5 ms |
| **Firm Real-Time** | Result discarded; no harm done | ECG monitor late-packet — dropped, no danger |
| **Soft Real-Time** | Degraded user experience | Bluetooth heart-rate stream to a phone app |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 1, 14-Mark Pattern]**

---

#### 🅐 Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a generic embedded system. Describe the function of *each* block. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Q. (b)** A sensor-node MCU executes a task in $4.5 \times 10^{6}$ cycles at a clock of $75\,\text{MHz}$ with an average CPI of 1.6. (i) Compute the execution time. (ii) If the firmware is optimized so that the instruction count drops by 30% *and* the clock is raised to $100\,\text{MHz}$, compute the new execution time and the speed-up obtained. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Draw the block diagram (see Section 4.1). Functions of each block:

- **Sensor:** Converts a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, light) into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, or frequency).
- **Signal Conditioning:** Amplifies, filters, and linearizes the raw sensor output. Typical circuits: instrumentation amp, anti-aliasing low-pass filter.
- **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Samples the conditioned analog signal and produces a binary word.
- **Processor (MCU/MPU/SoC):** Executes the embedded firmware — performs data fusion, control law, decision-making, communication protocol handling.
- **Memory (Flash + RAM):** Flash holds the program; RAM holds temporary variables, stack, heap.
- **Firmware / RTOS:** Bare-metal code, or a real-time OS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) that schedules tasks.
- **DAC / Driver / Actuator:** Converts digital decisions into analog drive signals (motors, LEDs, solenoids, valves).
- **Communication Interface:** UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB, BLE, Wi-Fi — exchanges data with external systems.
- **Power Management:** Battery, LDO, DC-DC converters, sleep modes.
- **Clock / Oscillator:** Crystal or MEMS oscillator providing the timebase.
- **User Interface:** Buttons, LCD, LEDs, touch panel.

*(Full credit if all 10 functional blocks are named and their roles are accurately described; the diagram carries at least 2 of the 7 marks.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Original execution time:**

$$T_1 = IC_1 \times CPI \times T_{clk,1} = \frac{IC_1 \times CPI}{f_{clk,1}}$$

$$T_1 = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{6} \times 1.6}{75 \times 10^{6}}\,\text{s} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{6}}{75 \times 10^{6}}\,\text{s}$$

$$T_1 = 0.096\,\text{s} = 96\,\text{ms}$$

**(ii) New execution time after optimization:**

New instruction count (30% drop):

$$IC_2 = (1 - 0.30) \times IC_1 = 0.70 \times 4.5 \times 10^{6} = 3.15 \times 10^{6}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{IC_2 \times CPI}{f_{clk,2}} = \frac{3.15 \times 10^{6} \times 1.6}{100 \times 10^{6}}\,\text{s}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{5.04 \times 10^{6}}{100 \times 10^{6}}\,\text{s} = 5.04 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{s} = 50.4\,\text{ms}$$

**Speed-up:**

$$S = \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{96}{50.4} = 1.905\times \approx 1.9\times$$

Cross-check: $S = \dfrac{1}{0.7} \times \dfrac{100}{75} = 1.4286 \times 1.3333 = 1.905$ ✓

---

#### 🅑 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Attempt*

> **Q. (a)** Explain the classification of embedded systems based on (i) performance/scale, and (ii) timing constraint. Give one practical example of each class. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Q. (b)** A wearable ECG SoC operates at $V_{DD} = 1.0\,\text{V}$, $f = 80\,\text{MHz}$, with switched capacitance $C = 8\,\text{nF}$ and activity factor $\alpha = 0.25$. (i) Calculate the dynamic power. (ii) The battery-life team proposes dropping $V_{DD}$ to $0.8\,\text{V}$ (with the same $f$, $\alpha$, $C$). Find the new dynamic power and the percentage power saved. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**(i) By performance/scale:**

| Class | Bit-width / Class | Typical Processor | Example |
|-------|-------------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Small-scale** | 8 / 16-bit MCU | ATmega328, PIC16, 8051 | TV remote, calculator |
| **Medium-scale** | 32-bit MCU/MPU | STM32, ESP32, ARM Cortex-M/A | Smartwatch, IoT gateway |
| **Large-scale** | SoC / FPGA | Zynq-7000, Snapdragon, Cyclone V | ADAS, base-station signal processing |
| **Ultra-large-scale** | Distributed multi-SoC | Cloud + edge | Avionics (F-35 has millions of lines of code spread across multi-core SoCs) |

**(ii) By timing constraint:**

- **Hard real-time** — missing deadline = catastrophe. Example: **airbag crash sensor / anti-lock braking system** in a car.
- **Firm real-time** — late result = discarded, no damage. Example: **stock-price tick display** in a trading terminal.
- **Soft real-time** — late result = degraded quality. Example: **MP3 audio playback** in a portable music player.

*(Marks are awarded for: 4 classes named, 4 correct examples for performance; 3 classes named, 3 correct examples for timing.)*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Dynamic power at 1.0 V:**

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$$

Substitute (SI units: $C = 8 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{F}$, $f = 80 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$):

$$P_{dyn} = (0.25)(8 \times 10^{-9})(1.0)^{2}(80 \times 10^{6})$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.25 \times 8 \times 80 \times 10^{-9+6}\,\text{W} = 160 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{dyn} = 160\,\text{mW}}$$

**(ii) Dynamic power at 0.8 V:**

$$P_{dyn}' = (0.25)(8 \times 10^{-9})(0.8)^{2}(80 \times 10^{6})$$

$$P_{dyn}' = 0.25 \times 8 \times 0.64 \times 80 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W}$$

$$P_{dyn}' = 102.4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W} = 102.4\,\text{mW}$$

**Percentage saved:**

$$\Delta P\,\% = \frac{160 - 102.4}{160} \times 100 = \frac{57.6}{160} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P\,\% = 36\,\%}$$

**Theoretical cross-check** (using $V$-squared proportionality):

$$\frac{P_{dyn}'}{P_{dyn}} = \left(\frac{0.8}{1.0}\right)^{2} = 0.64 \Rightarrow \text{Saving} = 36\% \;\checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up $V_{DD}^{2}$ with $V_{DD}$ scaling** — students often compute a *linear* 20% power saving for a 20% $V_{DD}$ drop. The relation is **quadratic**. A 20% voltage drop ⇒ **36% power saving** ($1 - 0.8^{2} = 0.36$).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert MHz to Hz** in the $T_{CPU}$ equation — silent off-by-10⁶ errors. Always state unit conversions explicitly.
> 3. **Conflating "real-time" with "fast"** — a system can be *fast* (low average latency) but *not real-time* (no deterministic worst-case deadline). In KTU valuation, "real-time" specifically means **bounded, deterministic response**, not "high performance".
> 4. **Writing "embedded system = IoT device"** — IoT is *one application class*; embedded systems also include standalone non-connected devices (microwave oven controller, ECU, washing machine).
> 5. **In Amdahl's law, dropping the serial-fraction term** — full-speed-up formula must include both $(1-P_s)$ *and* $P_s/N$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Embedded system = dedicated-purpose, software-driven, *integrated into* a larger device.
- ✅ **Core Triad:** **Sensor → Processor → Actuator** (this is the heart of PCCIT502).
- ✅ **Processor classes:** MCU, MPU, DSP, ASIC, FPGA, SoC — know at least one example of each.
- ✅ **Three-factor CPU time:** $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$. Memorize the units.
- ✅ **Dynamic power formula:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^{2} f$ — quadratic in $V_{DD}$.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P_s) + P_s / N}$; $S_{max} = 1/(1 - P_s)$ as $N \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Real-time classes:** Hard (catastrophic), Firm (discard), Soft (degrade) — examples mandatory.
- ✅ **Performance–Power–Cost Iron Triangle** — at most two of three can be optimized.
- ✅ **Availability:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$. For medical/avionics, $A > 0.999999$ ("five-nines" or more).
- ✅ **Design metrics to remember verbatim:** NRE cost, unit cost, performance, power, time-to-market, reliability, safety, availability.
- ✅ **Key distinction:** Embedded ≠ general-purpose ≠ IoT — be precise with terminology in written answers.
- ✅ **Hardware–software co-design** is the canonical methodology; *partitioning* is the most critical decision.
- ✅ **Common KTU numericals:** (a) CPU execution time, (b) $V_{DD}$–power tradeoff, (c) Amdahl's law, (d) per-unit cost over N units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Levels and Templates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT502 - SENSORS AND DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT Levels and Templates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an IoT "Level"?

In the context of IoT architecture, a **Level** defines the *degree of intelligence* and the *location of data processing* within the IoT system. As we ascend from **Level 1 to Level 5**, the system evolves from a simple "dump-to-cloud" sensor model to a fully distributed, intelligent, AI-enabled network where the edge device itself becomes capable of making autonomous decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Tone):**
> An **IoT Level** is a logical reference model that classifies an IoT system based on (a) the volume and type of data generated by sensors/actuators, (b) the layer where analytics is performed (sensors, edge, or cloud), and (c) the integration of advanced intelligence (AI/ML) on resource-constrained devices.

## 1.2 What is an IoT "Template"?

An **IoT Template** is a **reusable, abstract blueprint (metadata schema)** that defines the attributes, properties, commands, and semantics of a class of devices, services, or users. Templates enable **scalability** — instead of configuring every light bulb, one "Smart Light Template" is created and instantiated **N** times.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition (Analogy):**
> Think of IoT Levels as **"grades of a student"** — Grade 1 reads the book, Grade 3 understands it, Grade 5 writes a thesis on it. Similarly, **Level 1** just collects temperature data, **Level 3** processes it locally, and **Level 5** runs AI on the sensor itself.
>
> Think of IoT Templates as **"cooking recipes"** — the *Recipe* (Template) is abstract ("take 1 cup sugar, 2 cups flour"), but the *Cake* (Instantiated Device) is a real, physical object created by following that recipe. You can bake a hundred cakes from one recipe.

## 1.3 The 5 Levels of IoT (Standard KTU Reference Model)

| Level | Name | Intelligence Location | Core Function |
|:------|:-----|:---------------------|:--------------|
| **Level 1** | Basic Sensor-Cloud | Cloud only | Data acquisition + cloud storage |
| **Level 2** | Sensor-Cloud with Analytics | Cloud (basic) | Storage + preliminary descriptive analytics |
| **Level 3** | Sensor-Edge-Cloud | Edge + Cloud | Local pre-processing at the edge |
| **Level 4** | Connected Smart Network | Edge + Cloud + Peer-to-Peer | Smart objects collaborate as a network |
| **Level 5** | On-Device AI (Edge Intelligence) | Sensor/Edge (AI/ML) | Autonomous decision-making onboard |

## 1.4 The 8 Standard IoT Templates

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Templates are the backbone of any IoT middleware/platform** (e.g., AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT Core). KTU examiners often ask "List the templates and give one example of each."

| # | Template | What it Defines | Real-World Example |
|:-:|:---------|:----------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | **Device Template** | Class of physical devices (capabilities, commands) | Template for a *Smart AC* |
| 2 | **Service Template** | Server-side service behavior | "Send SMS" service template |
| 3 | **Resource Template** | Logical data object exposed by a device | A temperature *sensor resource* |
| 4 | **Application Template** | UI/UX layout and logic | Mobile app for *home automation* |
| 5 | **Product Template** | A group of devices sold as a unit | "Smart Home Starter Kit" |
| 6 | **User Template** | User roles, permissions, authentication | Admin user vs Guest user |
| 7 | **Group Template** | Logical grouping for batch operations | All lights in "Living Room" group |
| 8 | **Policy Template** | Security, alert, and action rules | "If temp > 50°C, trigger alarm" |

## 1.5 Key Terminology for the Board Exam

- **Instance vs. Template:** A *template* is the abstract schema; an *instance* is a real, addressable object.
- **Analytics Depth:** Describes the *intelligence* of data processing (descriptive → diagnostic → predictive → prescriptive).
- **Edge Computing:** Processing data *near* the source, reducing latency and bandwidth.
- **Fog Computing:** A distributed layer between edge and cloud (often synonymous with "Edge" in KTU textbooks).
- **Constrained Device:** A device with limited CPU, RAM, and power (e.g., a Class 0 device < 100 KB RAM).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deconstructing the 5 IoT Levels

### **LEVEL 1 — Sensor-Cloud Architecture (Data Acquisition)**

- **Sensors** collect raw analog signals (temperature, pressure, humidity).
- **Sensors' ADCs** convert analog → digital.
- A **gateway/router** (Wi-Fi/4G/LoRa) transmits the data via **TCP/IP/HTTP/MQTT**.
- The **cloud server** stores the data in time-series databases (e.g., InfluxDB, AWS Timestream).
- **No analytics** is performed at the sensor or gateway layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use Level 1?** Cheapest to deploy; best for high-frequency, non-critical logging (e.g., weather stations logging every minute).

### **LEVEL 2 — Sensor-Cloud with Preliminary Analytics**

- The **cloud server** now runs **basic descriptive analytics**: mean, min, max, percentiles, moving averages.
- Output is a **dashboard/visualization** for human operators.
- Still no real-time decision-making; analytics is *retrospective* (e.g., "Last week's average temperature was 28°C").

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Factory SCADA systems, environmental monitoring dashboards.

### **LEVEL 3 — Sensor-Edge-Cloud (The "Fog" Layer)**

- Introduces an **Edge/Fog node** (e.g., Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson Nano, industrial PLC) between sensors and cloud.
- Edge performs **pre-processing, filtering, and feature extraction**.
- Only *refined data* (not raw samples) is sent to the cloud → **bandwidth saved**.
- The system supports **realtime actuation**: e.g., turn off a motor the *instant* vibration exceeds threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** The *edge is not the sensor*. The edge is a **separate computing node** that aggregates multiple sensors.

### **LEVEL 4 — Connected Smart Object Network**

- Smart objects at the **edge form a peer-to-peer mesh network**.
- Devices can **discover, authenticate, and cooperate** with each other.
- Examples: Zigbee mesh of smart bulbs, Matter/Thread ecosystems, swarm of warehouse robots.
- The cloud becomes a **federator/orchestrator** rather than the primary data sink.

### **LEVEL 5 — On-Device AI / Edge Intelligence**

- Each **sensor node** (or near-sensor MCU) runs **TinyML** models (TensorFlow Lite Micro, ONNX Runtime, Edge Impulse).
- Decision-making happens **onboard**, with no human-in-the-loop.
- Cloud is used only for **model training and OTA firmware updates**.
- Examples: Predictive maintenance on a vibration sensor, on-device keyword spotting (Alexa wake-word), autonomous drones.

## 2.2 Deconstructing the 8 IoT Templates — Operational Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why templates?** Configuring 10,000 devices individually is impossible. Templates provide **inheritance and reusability** — like Object-Oriented Programming's *class-object* paradigm.

### 2.2.1 Device Template

A **Device Template** is a JSON/XML schema that defines:
- **Device identity** (serial number scheme, manufacturer)
- **Sensors/actuators present** (a list of *resource templates*)
- **Commands accepted** (e.g., `turnOn`, `setBrightness`)
- **Communication protocol** (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, LoRaWAN)

```text
Smart_AC_Device_Template
├── deviceId          : "AC_xxxx"
├── manufacturer      : "XYZ Ltd"
├── protocol          : "MQTT"
├── resources         : [Temp_Sensor, Humidity_Sensor, Compressor_Actuator]
├── commands          : [turnOn, turnOff, setTemp(16-30)]
└── firmwareVersion   : "1.2.0"
```

### 2.2.2 Service Template

Defines a **server-side cloud service** (notification engine, billing, AI inference API). It specifies inputs, outputs, and SLAs.

### 2.2.3 Resource Template

A **resource** is the smallest addressable data object (a single sensor reading, an actuator state). Resource templates define units, range, sampling rate, and data type.

| Field | Example Value |
|:------|:--------------|
| resourceName | `temperature` |
| dataType | `float` |
| unit | `°C` |
| range | $-40$ to $125$ |
| samplingRate | `1 Hz` |

### 2.2.4 Application Template

A **pre-built UI/UX layout** for mobile or web dashboards. Reduces development time for new deployments.

### 2.2.5 Product Template

A **commercial product** bundling multiple device templates. When a customer buys the product, all its device instances are auto-provisioned from the templates.

### 2.2.6 User Template

Specifies **user roles and privileges**: Admin (full control), Operator (limited commands), Viewer (read-only).

### 2.2.7 Group Template

Defines **dynamic groupings** of devices based on attributes (e.g., all devices in "Building A", all sensors with `firmwareVersion < 2.0`).

### 2.2.8 Policy Template

Defines **rule-based automation**: `IF condition THEN action`. Linked closely with **actuation logic** and alerts.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No closed-form mathematical formula exists for "IoT Levels"** — it is a *qualitative classification model*. However, several engineering formulas govern the **data flow, latency, and bandwidth** that justify the choice of levels.

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:--------------------|:-----|
| $T_{total}$ | End-to-end latency | $T_{total} = T_{sense} + T_{transmit} + T_{process} + T_{actuate}$ | $s$ |
| $B_{req}$ | Bandwidth required | $B_{req} = N \cdot S_{r} \cdot b$ | $bps$ |
| $T_{sense}$ | Sensor sampling time | $T_{sense} = \dfrac{1}{f_{s}}$ | $s$ |
| $E_{node}$ | Energy per node per cycle | $E_{node} = P_{proc} \cdot T_{proc} + P_{tx} \cdot T_{tx}$ | $J$ |
| $CF$ | Compression factor | $CF = \dfrac{\vert S_{raw} \vert}{\vert S_{compressed} \vert}$ | unitless |
| $SLA_{avail}$ | Availability | $SLA_{avail} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$ | $\%$ |

Where:
- $N$ = number of sensors, $S_{r}$ = sample rate (Hz), $b$ = bits per sample.
- $f_{s}$ = sampling frequency.
- $P_{proc}, P_{tx}$ = processing and transmission power.
- $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures, $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Utility:** These formulas are used in **Level-selection justification** — e.g., "We chose Level 3 (Edge) because bandwidth constraint $\vert B_{req} \vert$ exceeds the available link."

## 2.4 The Template Hierarchy (Inheritance Chain)

> [!NOTE]
> Templates follow a strict **parent-child inheritance** — a Product template inherits from multiple Device templates, which in turn inherit from Resource templates. This is analogous to **Java/C++ class inheritance** and is a high-yield KTU diagram question.

```text
Policy Template          User Template
        \                  /
         \                /
          Group Template
               |
          Product Template
               |
         Application Template
               |
          Service Template
               |
          Device Template
               |
         Resource Template  ←  (Atomic / Leaf level)
```

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Worked Example: Choosing the Correct IoT Level

### **Problem Statement (Modeled after KTU Numericals):**

> An industrial plant deploys **120 vibration sensors**, each sampling at $f_{s} = 5 \text{ kHz}$ with **16-bit** ADC resolution. The cloud link available has $B_{link} = 8 \text{ Mbps}$. Compute the required bandwidth and recommend an appropriate **IoT Level** for real-time fault detection.

---

### **Step 1 — Identify the given parameters**

We are given:
- $N = 120$ (number of sensors)
- $S_{r} = 5000 \text{ Hz}$ (sampling rate)
- $b = 16 \text{ bits}$ per sample
- $B_{link} = 8 \text{ Mbps} = 8 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps}$
- Requirement: **real-time** fault detection (latency-critical)

### **Step 2 — Compute raw bandwidth requirement using the KTU formula**

$$B_{req} = N \cdot S_{r} \cdot b$$

$$B_{req} = 120 \times 5000 \times 16$$

$$B_{req} = 9.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} = 9.6 \text{ Mbps}$$

### **Step 3 — Compare $B_{req}$ with $B_{link}$**

$$B_{req} = 9.6 \text{ Mbps} \quad \text{vs.} \quad B_{link} = 8 \text{ Mbps}$$

Since:

$$\dfrac{B_{req}}{B_{link}} = \dfrac{9.6}{8} = 1.2$$

The required bandwidth **exceeds** the available link by **20%**. Streaming raw data to the cloud is **NOT feasible**.

### **Step 4 — Determine the necessary compression factor**

To make it fit:

$$CF_{min} = \dfrac{B_{req}}{B_{link}} = 1.2$$

This means we must **compress** at least by a factor of **1.2** at the source.

### **Step 5 — Compute end-to-end latency for Level 1 (raw transmission)**

Assume:
- $T_{sense} = 0.1 \text{ ms}$
- $T_{transmit} = 5 \text{ ms}$
- $T_{process}$ (cloud) $= 20 \text{ ms}$
- $T_{actuate} = 1 \text{ ms}$

$$T_{total}^{L1} = 0.1 + 5 + 20 + 1 = 26.1 \text{ ms}$$

### **Step 6 — Compute end-to-end latency for Level 3 (edge processing)**

With edge pre-processing, $T_{transmit}$ is reduced because only features (not raw samples) are sent. Assume:
- $T_{transmit}^{edge} = 1 \text{ ms}$ (reduced payload)
- $T_{process}^{edge} = 2 \text{ ms}$ (local FFT/ML inference)

$$T_{total}^{L3} = 0.1 + 1 + 2 + 1 = 4.1 \text{ ms}$$

### **Step 7 — Decision & Justification**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** **Level 3 (Sensor-Edge-Cloud)** is the **correct choice**.
> - It satisfies the bandwidth constraint ($CF = 1.2$ achieved by edge compression).
> - It reduces $T_{total}$ by **84%** ($26.1 \to 4.1$ ms), enabling **real-time** fault detection.
> - If predictive maintenance is added later, the system can be **upgraded to Level 5** by deploying TinyML on the edge gateway.

### **Step 8 — Energy Analysis (Optional, KTU Higher-Mark Variant)**

Per-node energy per second, with $P_{proc} = 0.5 \text{ W}$ and $P_{tx} = 1.2 \text{ W}$:

$$E_{node} = (0.5 \times 0.002) + (1.2 \times 0.001) = 0.001 + 0.0012 = 0.0022 \text{ J/s}$$

For 120 nodes running for 1 hour:

$$E_{total} = 0.0022 \times 120 \times 3600 = 950.4 \text{ kJ}$$

This justifies why **battery-powered nodes** at **Level 5** would require energy harvesting.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Mapping a Real System to a Template

### **Problem:** Design the template hierarchy for a *Smart Classroom* containing lights, fans, and a projector.

### **Step 1 — Identify Resources (Atomic Level)**

| Resource Template | Attributes |
|:------------------|:-----------|
| `Light_Switch` | state (ON/OFF), brightness (0–100) |
| `Fan_Regulator` | speed (0–5), state (ON/OFF) |
| `Projector_HDMI` | inputSource, lampHours |

### **Step 2 — Create Device Templates**

```text
Device_Template: SmartLight_DT
  ├── resources: [Light_Switch]
  ├── commands: [turnOn, turnOff, setBrightness(0-100)]
  └── protocol: "MQTT/JSON"

Device_Template: SmartFan_DT
  ├── resources: [Fan_Regulator]
  └── commands: [setSpeed(0-5)]

Device_Template: Projector_DT
  ├── resources: [Projector_HDMI]
  └── commands: [powerOn, powerOff, switchInput]
```

### **Step 3 — Create Higher-Level Templates**

- **Group Template:** `Classroom_Group` containing 8 lights + 4 fans + 1 projector.
- **Product Template:** `SmartClassroom_Kit` (commercial bundle).
- **Application Template:** `TeacherApp` (UI with master ON/OFF).
- **User Template:** `TeacherRole` (full control), `StudentRole` (read-only).
- **Policy Template:** `AutoOff_Policy` → `IF time = 18:00 THEN turnOff all devices`.
- **Service Template:** `SMS_Alert_Service` → sends SMS to admin on projector fault.

### **Step 4 — Instantiate**

Each of the **40 classrooms** in the campus creates one **instance** of `Classroom_Group`, populated with real devices — without redefining the schema. This is the **power of templates**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. Define the term "IoT Level" and list the five levels with one-line descriptions.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **IoT Level** is a logical classification of an IoT system based on **where data is processed** (sensor / edge / cloud) and **how much intelligence** the system exhibits.

The five levels are:
1. **Level 1** — Sensor-Cloud: Raw data acquisition and cloud storage only.
2. **Level 2** — Sensor-Cloud with Analytics: Cloud performs descriptive analytics and dashboards.
3. **Level 3** — Sensor-Edge-Cloud: Edge node performs pre-processing, filtering, and local actuation.
4. **Level 4** — Connected Smart Object Network: Devices form a peer-to-peer collaborative mesh.
5. **Level 5** — On-Device AI: Sensors themselves run AI/ML models (TinyML) for autonomous decisions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write "Level 1 is best, Level 5 is worst" — it is the **opposite**. Higher level = higher intelligence. Marks are deducted for mis-ordering.

---

### **Q2. What is an IoT Template? Give two examples.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **IoT Template** is a **reusable abstract blueprint (schema)** that defines the attributes, resources, commands, and policies for a class of devices, services, or users. It enables **scalability, consistency, and rapid provisioning** across thousands of deployed devices.

**Two examples:**
- **Device Template:** A "SmartAC_DT" schema listing the temperature sensor, compressor actuator, and MQTT commands (`turnOn`, `setTemp`).
- **Policy Template:** A rule `IF temperature > 50°C THEN send alert AND shutdown compressor` applied to all motor devices in a factory.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question has **internal choice**. Solve **either Option A or Option B** in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### ✅ **OPTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q-A(a). Explain the five levels of IoT in detail with suitable diagrams. Compare Level 1 and Level 5.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **IoT Level** classification, formally defined in IoT reference architectures, describes the **progressive integration of intelligence** from raw data collection to fully autonomous on-device decision-making.

**Level 1 — Sensor-Cloud (1 mark):**
- Sensors + ADCs + Gateway + Cloud storage.
- No analytics. Used for **logging only** (e.g., weather data, energy meters).
- Bandwidth-heavy since raw samples are uploaded.

**Level 2 — Sensor-Cloud with Analytics (1 mark):**
- Cloud runs **descriptive analytics**: averages, min/max, histograms.
- Dashboards visualize historical trends. **No real-time actuation**.

**Level 3 — Sensor-Edge-Cloud (1 mark):**
- An **edge node** (Raspberry Pi, Jetson, PLC) performs local processing.
- Reduces bandwidth; enables **real-time actuation** at millisecond latency.
- Examples: Smart thermostats, industrial PLCs.

**Level 4 — Connected Smart Object Network (1 mark):**
- Devices form **mesh networks** (Zigbee, Thread, Matter).
- Peer-to-peer collaboration; cloud acts as a **federator**.
- Example: Smart lighting in a building where bulbs auto-adjust based on occupancy signals from neighbouring sensors.

**Level 5 — On-Device AI (1 mark):**
- Sensor nodes run **TinyML / Edge AI** models.
- **Fully autonomous** decision-making; cloud is used only for model training & OTA updates.
- Examples: Predictive maintenance, on-device keyword spotting, autonomous drones.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Level 1 | Level 5 |
|:----------|:--------|:--------|
| Intelligence | None (storage only) | AI/ML onboard |
| Latency | High (100s of ms) | Ultra-low (<10 ms) |
| Bandwidth | Very high | Negligible |
| Power | Low | High (needs SoC + ML accelerator) |
| Cost | Lowest | Highest |
| Privacy | Low (raw data uploaded) | High (data stays local) |

---

#### **Q-A(b). With a neat diagram, explain the 8 IoT Templates. How do they help in scalability?** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Templates are **abstract, reusable schemas** in JSON/XML that define classes of devices, services, resources, etc. They form the **configuration backbone** of any IoT platform (AWS IoT, Azure IoT, Google Cloud IoT).

**The 8 Templates (4 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Device Template** — Defines capabilities, commands, protocol of a device class (e.g., `SmartAC_DT`).
2. **Service Template** — Defines cloud service behavior (e.g., `NotificationService_ST`).
3. **Resource Template** — Smallest data object schema (e.g., `temperature` resource, float, °C, range).
4. **Application Template** — Pre-built UI/UX layout (e.g., `FactoryDashboard_AT`).
5. **Product Template** — Commercial bundle of multiple device templates.
6. **User Template** — Roles & permissions (Admin, Operator, Viewer).
7. **Group Template** — Logical device grouping (e.g., all sensors on Floor 2).
8. **Policy Template** — IF-THEN rules for automation and alerts.

**Template Hierarchy Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.)*

**Scalability Justification (1 mark):**
- Configuring 10,000 devices individually would take months and cause errors.
- With templates, **one schema is created once** and instantiated N times → **provisioning in minutes**.
- Updates (firmware, commands) propagate via the template → **centralized maintenance**.
- Inheritance allows building complex products from simple device templates → **modularity**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often **list only 4-5 templates** and lose 2-3 marks. The full set of **8 templates** is mandatory for full marks. Also, avoid spelling it as "Templete" or "Tamplate" — both are flagged.

---

### ✅ **OPTION B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

#### **Q-B(a). Differentiate between Edge Computing and Cloud Computing in the context of IoT Levels. Justify why Level 3 is often preferred over Level 2 in industrial IoT.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Cloud Computing:** Centralized, virtually unlimited compute/storage, located in remote data centres. Accessed via the internet with **latency in 100s of ms**.
- **Edge Computing:** Distributed computing performed **near the data source** (on gateways, PLCs, or smart devices). Latency in **single-digit ms**.

**Detailed Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cloud Computing | Edge Computing |
|:----------|:----------------|:---------------|
| Location | Remote data centre | Near sensor / on device |
| Latency | 100–500 ms | 1–10 ms |
| Bandwidth | Requires high uplink | Minimal (only features sent) |
| Compute Power | Virtually unlimited | Constrained (SoC class) |
| Real-time Capability | Poor | Excellent |
| Cost Model | Pay-per-use, OPEX | CAPEX + maintenance |
| Failure Mode | Internet outage breaks system | Operates offline |
| Privacy | Data leaves premises | Data stays local |
| Use Case | Long-term analytics, training | Real-time actuation |

**Why Level 3 is Preferred Over Level 2 in Industrial IoT (2 marks):**
1. **Latency-critical actuation:** Industrial processes (e.g., emergency shutdown of a motor) cannot tolerate 500 ms cloud round-trips. Level 3's edge node responds in <10 ms.
2. **Bandwidth economics:** Streaming vibration data from 120 sensors at 5 kHz (as in our Section 3.1 calculation, $9.6 \text{ Mbps}$) is economically infeasible. Edge pre-processing reduces this by 10–100×.
3. **Reliability:** Factories cannot afford cloud outages. Edge operates offline.
4. **Data sovereignty:** Proprietary process data must remain on-premise for IP protection.
5. **Predictive maintenance:** Edge nodes can run lightweight ML (Level 5 hybrid) for incipient fault detection.

---

#### **Q-B(b). Design the complete template hierarchy for a Smart Campus with at least 6 templates. Show instantiation for one classroom.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Domain (1 mark):**
A Smart Campus has classrooms equipped with smart lights, fans, projectors, ACs, and door locks. Each classroom is monitored and controlled by faculty and admins.

**Step 2 — Resource Templates (1 mark):**

```text
Resource_Template: Light_Switch_RT
  resourceName: "light"
  dataType: "boolean"
  commands: [turnOn, turnOff, setBrightness]

Resource_Template: Fan_Speed_RT
  resourceName: "fanSpeed"
  dataType: "integer"
  range: [0, 5]
```

**Step 3 — Device Templates (1 mark):**

```text
Device_Template: SmartLight_DT
  inherits: [Light_Switch_RT]
  protocol: "MQTT/JSON"

Device_Template: Projector_DT
  resources: [inputSource, lampHours]
  commands: [powerOn, powerOff]
```

**Step 4 — Service Template (0.5 mark):**
`SMS_Alert_ST` — sends SMS to admin when projector lamp exceeds 3000 hours.

**Step 5 — Application Template (0.5 mark):**
`FacultyApp_AT` — Mobile UI with classroom selector and master switches.

**Step 6 — Group Template (0.5 mark):**
`Classroom_Group_GT` — Contains 8 lights + 4 fans + 1 projector + 1 AC.

**Step 7 — Policy Template (0.5 mark):**
`AutoShut_PT` — `IF time == 18:30 THEN turnOff all Classroom_Group devices`.

**Step 8 — User Template (0.5 mark):**
- `AdminRole_UT` — Full control
- `FacultyRole_UT` — Single-classroom control
- `StudentRole_UT` — Read-only

**Step 9 — Instantiation for Classroom 301 (1 mark):**

```text
Instance: Classroom_301
  group: Classroom_Group_GT
  devices:
    - { id: "L301_A1", template: SmartLight_DT, state: OFF }
    - { id: "L301_A2", template: SmartLight_DT, state: OFF }
    - { id: "F301_1", template: SmartFan_DT, speed: 0 }
    - { id: "P301", template: Projector_DT, lampHours: 1240 }
  policy: AutoShut_PT
  users: [faculty_cse301, admin_campus]
```

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
This template-driven design allows the **same schema** to be instantiated for **all 40 classrooms** in under 10 minutes — demonstrating the scalability benefit of templates.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often **skip the Instantiation step** and present only abstract templates. KTU specifically allocates **at least 1 mark for showing a concrete instance**. Without it, you cap at 6/7.
